title: Carved ash butter spreader
subtitle: Green-ash flat spreader for butter and soft cheese
excerpt: A flat-bladed spreader in green ash. Straight handle, gentle taper to a rounded tip. A first project for new carvers who find a full spoon bowl too steep; the flat blade builds knife-control with push and thumb-pivot cuts alone.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for the axe-and-knife treen progression. Beeton's Book of Household Management on household treen.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture. Carves more easily than seasoned wood under hand tools.
A short length of round or split timber, ready to be worked into a blank for carving.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short controlled cut pivoted by the off-hand thumb on the back of the blade. Used for fine shaping where blade-travel is small.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.

content.text: A butter spreader is the flattest piece of treen a carver can make. There is no bowl to hollow; the blade is a thin flat lozenge tapering from handle to tip. Ash carves cleanly in
green
condition, dries pale and food-safe, and the straight grain follows cuts with little tendency to tear. A half-evening project for a beginner.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
A green ash
billet

: 22 cm long, 4 cm across, 2 cm deep. Ash is straight-grained and dries food-safe. Windfall ash is common in the UK; tree-surgeon offcuts are reliable. Sycamore is a direct substitute with a finer, paler grain.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
for all the shaping; a carving axe for rough-shaping the blank (optional); a leather strop; raw linseed oil. No hook knife needed.
Method

Mark the spreader outline on the flat face of the billet: 14 cm of handle narrowing from 2.5 cm at the shoulder to 1.5 cm at the end, then a blade section 8 cm long widening to 3 cm at its widest point, tapering to a rounded tip. Mark the same outline on both flat faces.
If using an axe, rough off the waste from the blade taper and the handle profile, working from the shoulder outward toward each end. Leave 5 mm proud of the pencil line.
Work the handle to a smooth oval cross-section with
push cuts
: blade angled at 30 degrees to the long axis, lifting thin curls. Aim for 12 mm thick and 16 mm wide.
Taper the blade section on both faces equally, from 5 mm thick at the shoulder to 2 mm at the tip. Use
thumb-pivot cuts
for the curved shoulder transition.
Round the tip with small thumb-pivot cuts on both faces, working toward the end. Check the outline by placing the spreader flat on the bench and sighting from above.
Leave to dry on a shelf for 10 days, out of direct sun and away from a heat source.
Finishing
Sand through 120 and 180 grit along the grain. Apply raw linseed oil liberally, let it soak in for one hour, wipe off the surplus. The cure time before food contact is 2 weeks.
Variations
Sycamore or cherry instead of ash: same method. Cherry develops a warmer pink tone with age.
Wider blade for soft cheese or pate: widen the blade section to 4 cm, keeping the same taper profile.
Left-handed version: the profile is symmetrical, so no adjustment is needed.
Care
Rinse under warm water after use, towel-dry immediately. Never dishwasher or long soak. Re-oil when the wood looks dry.

items.symptom: Blade surface tears across the face
items.cause: Cutting against the grain on the wide flat face
items.fix: Read the end-grain before cutting the face. Cut in the direction where the grain lines run down into the surface, not up out of it.

items.symptom: Blade is thicker at one edge than the other
items.cause: Unequal cut depth across the width
items.fix: Place the blade on the bench and sight along the edge from the handle end. Take a light push cut from the high side.

items.cause: The tip was thinned too much before the blank dried
items.fix: Leave at least 3 mm at the tip during the green-carving stage. Final thinning to 2 mm after drying.
